name: Bird ID: Eastern North America
description: Identify 50 of the most common backyard, park, and roadside birds of eastern North America from photos. Details give the scientific name, key field marks, and how to separate lookalikes.

fields.extra: Cardinalis cardinalis. Male brilliant red with a black face mask, pointed crest, and thick orange-red bill; female warm buff with red tinges in wings, tail, and crest.
Cyanocitta cristata. Blue above with a crest, black necklace, and white wing bars and tail corners. Loud, bold corvid; often mimics hawk calls.
Turdus migratorius. Brick-red breast, dark gray back, yellow bill, broken white eye-ring. Runs and stops on lawns hunting worms.
Zenaida macroura. Slim buffy-tan dove with black wing spots and a long pointed tail edged in white. Wings whistle on takeoff; song is a mournful cooing.
Spinus tristis. Breeding male lemon-yellow with black cap and black wings; winter birds drab olive-brown. Bouncy, dipping flight; loves thistle seed.
Poecile atricapillus. Black cap and bib, white cheeks, buffy flanks. In the Southeast replaced by the nearly identical Carolina chickadee (smaller, less white in wing).
Baeolophus bicolor. Gray above and pale below with a jaunty gray crest, big black eye, and peach-washed flanks. Frequent feeder visitor; whistles 'peter-peter-peter'.
Sitta carolinensis. Blue-gray back, white face and breast, black cap. Creeps headfirst DOWN tree trunks, unlike woodpeckers and creepers.
Thryothorus ludovicianus. Warm rusty-brown wren with a bold white eyebrow stripe and cocked tail. Huge voice for its size: ringing 'teakettle-teakettle-teakettle'.
Dryobates pubescens. Tiny black-and-white woodpecker; male has a red nape patch. Bill is stubby, much shorter than the head, which separates it from the near-identical hairy woodpecker.
Dryobates villosus. Looks like a large downy woodpecker, but the bill is nearly as long as the head and the outer tail feathers are pure white, unspotted.
Melanerpes carolinus. Zebra-barred back with a red crown and nape; the red belly wash is faint. Often misnamed 'red-headed' - that species has an entirely crimson head.

Colaptes auratus. Brown woodpecker with black barring, a black chest crescent, and spotted belly; white rump flashes in flight. Eastern birds show yellow wing linings; often feeds on the ground on ants.
Dryocopus pileatus. Crow-sized, mostly black, with a flaming red crest and bold white neck stripes. Excavates big rectangular holes in dead wood.
Melanerpes erythrocephalus. Entire head and neck crimson, snow-white body, black back with big white wing patches. The red-bellied woodpecker, by contrast, has red only on the crown and nape.
Sialia sialis. Royal-blue above with a rusty-orange throat and breast and white belly. Perches on wires and fence posts, dropping to the ground for insects; nests in boxes.
Corvus brachyrhynchos. All-black, fan-shaped tail, familiar 'caw-caw'. The common raven is much larger with a wedge-shaped tail, shaggy throat, and croaking call.
Sturnus vulgaris. Chunky and short-tailed; glossy purple-green in summer with a yellow bill, white-spangled in winter. Introduced from Europe in 1890; flies straight and fast, forms huge flocks.
Passer domesticus. Male has a gray crown, black bib, and chestnut nape; female plain gray-brown with a buffy eye stripe. Old World sparrow tied to buildings, not related to native sparrows.

Haemorhous mexicanus. Male has red on the head and breast with brown-streaked flanks and belly; female plain-faced and streaky. Purple finch males are raspberry-red over the whole head and back with little flank streaking.

Haemorhous purpureus. Male looks dipped in raspberry juice, including the back; female boldly striped head with a white eyebrow. House finch red is more orange and confined to head and chest, with streaky flanks.
Junco hyemalis. Eastern 'slate-colored' form is smooth gray with a white belly, pink bill, and white outer tail feathers that flash in flight. A classic winter 'snowbird' under feeders.
Melospiza melodia. Brown-streaked sparrow whose breast streaks converge into a central dark spot; long rounded tail pumped in flight. Sings persistently from low perches.
Zonotrichia albicollis. Crisp white throat patch, yellow spot between eye and bill, striped crown. Whistles 'Oh-sweet-Canada-Canada'; scratches under feeders in winter.
Spizella passerina. Small, clean gray breast (no streaks), bright rufous cap, and black line through the eye. Song is a dry mechanical trill on one pitch.
Pipilo erythrophthalmus. Male black above with rufous flanks and white belly; female chocolate-brown. Scratches noisily in leaf litter; song 'drink-your-teeeea'.
Mimus polyglottos. Slim gray bird with a long tail and white wing patches that flash in flight. Endlessly repeats imitated phrases, each usually 3+ times; sings even at night.
Dumetella carolinensis. Uniform slate-gray with a black cap and rusty patch under the tail. Named for its whiny, cat-like mew call; skulks in dense shrubs.
Toxostoma rufum. Rich rufous above, heavily dark-streaked below, long tail, yellow eye, slightly curved bill. Sings phrases in pairs, unlike the mockingbird's triples.
Bombycilla cedrorum. Sleek fawn-brown crest, black mask, yellow tail tip, and waxy red wingtip spots. Travels in tight flocks stripping berry trees; call a thin high trill.
Agelaius phoeniceus. Male glossy black with red-and-yellow shoulder patches; female looks like a big dark-streaked sparrow. Sings 'conk-la-ree!' from cattails.
Quiscalus quiscula. Lanky iridescent blackbird with a bronze body sheen, purple-blue head, pale yellow eye, and long keel-shaped tail.
Molothrus ater. Male glossy black with a chocolate-brown head and stubby finch-like bill; female plain gray-brown. Brood parasite: lays eggs in other songbirds' nests.
Icterus galbula. Male flame-orange with a full black hood and black wings with white bars; female yellow-orange. Weaves a hanging pouch nest; comes to orange halves and jelly.
Pheucticus ludovicianus. Male black and white with a rose-red triangle on the breast and a massive pale bill; female like a big striped sparrow with a bold white eyebrow.
Passerina cyanea. Breeding male entirely deep blue with a small silver conical bill; female plain warm brown. Sings paired phrases from wires and treetops all summer.
Piranga olivacea. Breeding male blood-red with jet-black wings and tail; female olive-yellow with dusky wings. A canopy bird of mature deciduous forest, more often heard than seen.
Archilochus colubris. The only breeding hummingbird in most of the East. Male has an iridescent ruby gorget that can look black; both sexes emerald-green above.
Sayornis phoebe. Drab gray-brown flycatcher, darkest on the head, with no strong wing bars or eye-ring. Constantly wags its tail downward; says its own name, 'fee-bee'. Nests under eaves and bridges.
Tachycineta bicolor. Iridescent steel-blue-green above, clean snow-white below, with a slightly notched tail. Nests in boxes and tree cavities near water.
Hirundo rustica. Cobalt-blue above with a rusty throat, buff-orange underparts, and a deeply forked 'swallow tail'. Builds open mud-cup nests on beams and ledges.
Chaetura pelagica. A 'cigar with wings': dark, tiny-tailed, with stiff bowed wings and twinkling flight, never perching in the open. Chatters overhead all summer; roosts in chimneys.
Charadrius vociferus. A plover of lawns and gravel lots: two black breast bands, orange rump in flight, piercing 'kill-deer' cry. Famous for its broken-wing distraction display.
Anas platyrhynchos. Male has a glossy green head, white neck ring, and chestnut breast; female mottled brown with an orange-and-black bill. Both show a blue wing patch (speculum).
Branta canadensis. Black head and neck with a white chinstrap, brown body. Grazes on lawns and golf courses; flies in honking V formations.
Ardea herodias. Huge blue-gray heron with a black head plume and dagger bill; stands motionless in shallows, then strikes. Flies with neck folded in an S, legs trailing.
Meleagris gallopavo. Huge dark game bird with bronze iridescence and a bare blue-and-red head; males strut with fanned tails. Roosts in trees at night.
Cathartes aura. Soars for hours in a wobbly, teetering V (dihedral); two-toned underwings, tiny bare red head. Finds carrion by smell.
Buteo jamaicensis. Bulky soaring hawk of roadsides; adults show a brick-red tail and a dark 'belly band' across the white chest. Its raspy scream is Hollywood's all-purpose raptor cry.

Astur cooperii (until 2023 Accipiter cooperii). Crow-sized bird hunter with short rounded wings, a long banded tail with a rounded tip, and red eyes; adults blue-gray above with rusty-barred chest. Ambushes feeders.
